text: Approaches to the education of the gift within modern nation-states are highly divergent. Although a number of countries have established sophisticated and specialized strategies to further the talents of the gifted few, other countries do not acknowledge the concept of the gifted as an educational priority and, therefore, usually have to depend on a combination of state school provision and enrichment programs run from a variety of other bases in the community. This commonplace misconception of gifted education as either elitist, or as being one way in which highly able children can be educated as effectively as possible without special provision, acts as a constraint to realizing fully the potential of children whatever their talents. An important part of a holistic approach to addressing the complexities inherent within the field of giftedness is to acknowledge the range of pedagogical and organisational conditions, provided through differing educational systems, which exist between countries and can be identified within European contexts. This article forms part of a wider investigation into the issues and problems occurring within the field of the education of the gift.
